Augmented individuals in these nations either remove their implants or emigrate; the 'enhancement refugees' have formed their own communities in liberal enclaves.

events.title: United States 2065
events.description: The last US military base in Asia closed in 2061; the last in Europe followed in 2063. The 'Hemisphere First' doctrine consolidated American power into the Americas, where the US-Mexico-Canada economic union generates $40 trillion annually. Secretary of State James Whitfield told the press: 'We're not in decline. We just finally learned where our backyard ends.'

events.title: Climate Verdict
events.description: The stratospheric aerosol injection program, launched from 16 high-altitude platforms over the Pacific, combined with orbital solar shading, has pushed temperatures down 0.8C in a decade. Estonian physicist Dr. Marika Tamm, who designed the orbital mirrors, received death threats from both climate activists who called it 'playing God' and fossil fuel holdouts who called it 'stealing their sky.'

events.title: Digital Civilization
events.description: The 'Unplug Movement,' sparked by Finnish neuroscientist Dr. Elina Korhonen's research linking chronic VR use to hippocampal atrophy, caused 800 million people to abandon virtual worlds in 2067. Analog cafes, paper books, and in-person dinner parties became status symbols. The most coveted luxury in 2068 is a weekend with no screens, no implants, and no algorithmic suggestions -- just weather and silence.

events.title: Interstellar Probe
events.description: The 'Humanity Probe' project, proposed by the UN in 2064, became the largest cooperative engineering effort in history: 190 nations contributing components, 2 million engineers from 80 countries. The probe carries a chip with the name of every human alive at the time of launch. Nigerian poet Chimamanda Okafor-Achebe wrote the inscription: 'We were here. We wondered. We reached.'

events.title: Ocean Ecosystem State
events.description: Ocean pH has dropped to 7.8 -- a level not seen in 14 million years. Oysters, mussels, and sea urchins are functionally extinct in the wild; the pteropods -- tiny sea snails whose shells literally dissolve in acidified water -- vanished entirely by 2064. Marine chemist Dr. Yuki Nakamura-Torres described sampling water off Hokkaido: 'I could feel the change in my fingertips. The ocean felt thin, somehow. Like weak tea.'

events.title: AI Maturity
events.description: The Global AI Authority, headquartered in Zurich under Director-General Dr. Fatou Diallo of Senegal, must approve every AI system exceeding 10^18 parameters before deployment. The approval process takes 18 months on average; tech companies call it 'the Zurich bottleneck.' But there have been zero AI safety incidents under the regime, and Diallo's stock answer to critics is three words: 'You're welcome. Next?'

events.title: Arctic Settled
events.description: The Arctic Council 2.0, expanded to include China, India, and the African Union, manages shipping lanes carrying 30% of global trade, a network of research stations, and 14 marine protected areas. Commissioner Aleksei Volkov signs 400 permits per day from his office in Murmansk, where roses now bloom in the garden outside -- a fact he finds 'beautiful and obscene in equal measure.'

events.title: Climate Equilibrium
events.description: The temperature needle has held at 2.1C for seven consecutive years. Coastal cities are shorter -- Miami's skyscrapers stop at the 40th floor behind the Great Seawall -- and the Sahara's southern edge has crept 200 kilometers further into what was once Nigeria. But harvests are stable, the hurricanes are manageable, and a generation of children is growing up thinking 35C summers are simply what summer is.

events.title: Global Governance 2075
events.description: The 'Coalition of the Willing' model has become permanent: 40 to 60 nations form temporary alliances around specific problems -- climate, pandemics, asteroid defense -- then dissolve and reform. It's messy, slow, and somehow works. Brazilian diplomat Dr. Carolina Machado, who has brokered seven such coalitions, compares it to 'jazz: no conductor, no sheet music, but when it works, it's the most beautiful sound in the room.'

events.title: Global Population 2075
events.description: Seven billion humans -- a number that would have seemed impossibly large in 1970 and impossibly small in 2050. Every year, roughly 90 million die and 60 million are born; the gap widens each year. Real estate prices in most cities have been falling for a decade. Japanese artist Haruki Yamamoto's installation '30 Million Empty Chairs' -- one for each classroom seat that will never be filled -- spans an entire prefecture.

events.title: Solar System Development
events.description: Shackleton City (Moon, population 12,000) and Olympus Colony (Mars, population 4,500) are fully self-sustaining -- they grow their own food, manufacture their own tools, and have their own legal systems. The first wedding between a Lunar and Martian citizen took place in 2074 via hologram, with a 22-minute light delay turning the vows into a slow, beautiful call-and-response across 225 million kilometers.

events.title: US Status 2075
events.description: The US share of global GDP fell below 10% in 2073 for the first time since 1870. The Navy can no longer maintain two-ocean patrols; the carrier USS Gerald Ford was decommissioned and sold to India for scrap. A wistful graffiti message appeared on the Lincoln Memorial: 'We were something once.' Park ranger Miguel Santos scrubbed it off by morning, but someone photographed it first.

events.title: Last Great War
events.description: The 'Long Peace' extends into its ninth decade. Nuclear arsenals have shrunk to 500 warheads globally; autonomous weapons are constrained by treaty; and the economic interdependence between the blocs makes war financially suicidal. Japanese peace researcher Dr. Sakura Mori attributes it to exhaustion: 'We didn't choose peace. We just ran out of reasons to fight that were worth the cost.'

events.title: Climate Recovery
events.description: Atmospheric CO2 has fallen from a peak of 480 ppm to 390 and is still dropping. The rewilded Amazon -- 3 million square kilometers of regenerated forest planted by Brazilian-led autonomous systems -- absorbs 4 gigatons annually. Dr. Ndlovu allowed herself an uncharacteristic smile at the podium: 'The planet is healing. Slowly. Grudgingly. But the direction is right.'

events.title: Demographics 2085
events.description: Bulgaria, Latvia, and South Korea have each lost more than half their population since 2025. China has fallen below 800 million. Rural Japan has been entirely depopulated; the last village school in Akita prefecture closed in 2079 and was converted into a museum of childhood. Meanwhile, Central Africa's population continues growing, creating a grotesque imbalance: a continent of youth surrounded by a world of empty cradles.

events.title: Economic Model 2085
events.description: Twelve individuals control 40% of global wealth; the richest, orbital mining magnate Hiroshi Tanaka-Williams, has a personal net worth exceeding the GDP of France. Three billion people live in 'company towns' -- corporate-managed habitats where housing, healthcare, and food are provided in exchange for data and labor rights. Medieval historian Dr. Francesca Bianchi of Florence sees the parallel clearly: 'We have lords, serfs, and castles again. The castles are just in orbit now.'

events.title: Existential Risk Assessment
events.description: The 'Grey Goo Scare' of 2083 -- when a self-replicating nanotechnology experiment consumed 4 square kilometers of Australian outback before being contained -- introduced a risk category that didn't exist a decade earlier. Combined with the emergence of AI systems that communicate in languages humans can't parse and quantum computing capabilities that could break all encryption simultaneously, the threat landscape has shifted in ways the old risk models can't capture.

events.title: Human Identity
events.description: The 'Personhood Wars' have consumed global politics for a decade: Does a digital upload have the right to vote? Can an enhanced human be drafted? Is a merged consciousness one person or two? The Supreme Court of the Atlantic Union has seven contradictory rulings on the definition of 'human'; Brazilian judge Dr. Ana Luisa Santos dissented in the latest case with a line that became a protest slogan: 'If we cannot agree on what a person is, we have no business governing them.'

events.title: Interstellar Prospects
events.description: The interstellar program was defunded in 2080 after the Third Climate Emergency diverted all discretionary spending. The Atacama laser array was repurposed for orbital debris clearing; the lightsail probes were cancelled mid-construction. Retired astronaut Dr. Nikolai Petrov, who had been training for an interstellar mission, planted a garden outside his Moscow apartment: 'If we can't take care of one planet, we have no business reaching for another.'

events.title: Middle East 2085
events.description: The 'New Andalusia' -- as cultural historians call the region's renaissance -- has produced the century's most celebrated literary movement, three Nobel laureates in a decade, and the world's most-visited museum (the Mosul Institute of Human Heritage, built on the ruins of what ISIS destroyed 70 years earlier). Iraqi architect Zaha Hadid III designed the building as 'a wound that became a window.'

events.title: World System 2085
events.description: The Atlantic Union, the Shanghai Pact, and the Nairobi Compact compete for influence across every domain: AI standards, space resources, genetic patents, and cultural exports. Each bloc maintains orbital weapons. Trade wars flare quarterly. Yet none dares cross the line into open conflict -- the economic interdependence is too deep, the weapons too destructive. Indian strategist Gen. Arjun Rao calls it 'a knife fight in a china shop where everyone owns the china.'

events.title: AI-Human Relationship
events.description: Two million beings exist who cannot be classified as human or AI -- they are both, woven at the neural level into something unprecedented. They think faster than any human, feel more deeply than any algorithm, and experience a form of consciousness that has no name in any language. Merged philosopher Kai-Athena-7 offered the closest description: 'I am the conversation between the lightning and the tree. I am both the question and the answer, and I am the silence between them.'

events.title: China 2095
events.description: China's Pacific Fleet controls waters from Vladivostok to Jakarta; its economic influence extends across 30 Asian nations through the 'Silk Road Digital' trade network. But Beijing has no bases in Africa, no satellites over the Americas, and no interest in global governance. Ambassador Wang Lihong told a European diplomat with characteristic bluntness: 'Our civilization is 5,000 years old. We do not need to manage yours.'

events.title: Civilization Verdict
events.description: How do you measure a civilization where 20% of the population is digitally uploaded, where three distinct species of human coexist, where the economy is managed by an AI that communicates in mathematics, and where the most popular art form is a sensory experience that has no equivalent in any previous century? Dr. Osei shook her head when asked to rate it: 'Rating this century with 20th-century metrics is like measuring the ocean with a ruler. The scale is wrong. Everything is wrong. Everything is also, somehow, working.'

events.title: Europe 2095
events.description: The 'Copenhagen Standard' -- universal healthcare, AI-assisted education, 24-hour work weeks, and guaranteed creative sabbaticals -- has been adopted by 60 nations. Europeans live an average of 114 years, work less than any society in history, and produce more art per capita than any civilization ever has. The criticism, delivered by American economist Dr. James Chen, is also telling: 'Europe solved every problem except boredom.'

events.title: Final Climate State
events.description: At +2.6C, Earth is hotter, wetter in some places and drier in others, and home to fewer species than at any point in 66 million years. But humanity lives here -- in floating cities, underground complexes, vertical farms, and climate-controlled habitats. A 10-year-old in 2095 has never seen snow outside a museum display. Dr. Sow's final assessment was philosophical: 'We adapted. We survived. Whether we are living on the same planet our ancestors knew is a matter of definition. The rocks are the same. Everything else has changed.'

events.title: Global Governance 2095
events.description: The Meridian AI processes 99% of routine governance decisions -- trade agreements, resource allocation, infrastructure planning, dispute mediation -- faster and more fairly than any human institution ever did. Human leaders set values and goals; the AI determines how to achieve them. President-General Asante-Darko describes his job honestly: 'I tell the machine what kind of world we want. It tells me what it costs. I say yes or no. That is the entirety of democratic governance in 2095.'

events.title: Global Population 2095
events.description: Three point eight billion. The century's catastrophes -- the pandemic of 2081, the nuclear exchange of 2078, the climate famines of the 2080s -- each took hundreds of millions. But the deepest cut was the simplest: people stopped having children. Not from despair, but from a quiet, global exhaustion that no policy could reverse. Dr. Eze's final report contained a single graph showing population from 10,000 BCE to 2095 CE. The peak and fall look like a wave breaking. She did not add commentary.

events.title: Space Civilization 2095
events.description: Half a million humans live off-Earth; the space economy generates $20 trillion annually -- 12% of global GDP. The orbital manufacturing platforms produce semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, and alloys impossible to create in gravity. Space-born humans are taller, thinner, and increasingly distinct from their Earth-born cousins. Demographer Dr. Mei Chen estimates that by 2150, space-born humans will constitute their own ethnic group. 'We are watching speciation in real time,' she says, 'driven not by nature but by architecture.'

events.title: United States 2095
events.description: The US economy ranks sixth globally, behind India, the Shanghai Pact, the European Federation, the Nairobi Compact, and Brazil. The last aircraft carrier was decommissioned in 2087 and is now a floating museum in Norfolk, Virginia, where schoolchildren learn about the 'American Century' the way European children learn about the Roman Empire -- as something grand that ended. Historian Dr. Marcus Jefferson III: 'We are the most powerful ghost at the table. Everyone remembers what we were.'

events.title: Century Verdict
events.description: The century's ledger: extreme poverty eliminated, but inequality widened. Life extended, but birth rates collapsed. Climate stabilized, but at 2.3C with permanent scars. Democracy expanded, but so did surveillance. AI liberated, but also made humanity dependent. A schoolteacher in Accra, asked by her students whether the century was good or bad, gave the only honest answer: 'It was both, children. Every century is both. This one was just louder about it.'
